title: Support Matrix Audit
tagline: Four minutes to see who's aligned with the change you're driving — and who isn't.

children.text: Every senior change comes down to four kinds of people. This map shows you who you have, who you're missing, and — most usefully — which of your supporters quietly aren't going to back you when it matters.
Names you enter stay in your browser. Only initials are sent when you submit your email at the end.

questions.changePromptLabel: Name the change you're trying to drive.
questions.changePromptHelp: One sentence. The map is most useful when it's mapping the support around a specific thing, not your career in general.
changePromptExamples: Restructure customer ops around AI tooling by Q3
Move into a senior leadership role within 12 months
Reset our technology investment plan to reflect the last year
Get sign-off on the platform consolidation by Q3

stanceOptions.label: Engaged
stanceOptions.description: Actively engaging with AI, has views, deploying tools

stanceOptions.label: Cautious
stanceOptions.description: Engaged but with reservations they articulate

stanceOptions.label: Sceptical
stanceOptions.description: Actively wary, or hasn't moved past surface awareness

stanceOptions.label: Unknown
stanceOptions.description: You genuinely don't know their stance

children.text: You don't have a champion. This is the most common gap I see in senior coaching, and the one that quietly kills most initiatives. The rest of the map matters less than this gap does.

Your map has the structural ingredients for success — but the most specific risk in it is the one your strongest champion presents on AI. Read the diagnostic blocks below in order. The champion conversation is the one to have first.
Allies in abundance, no champion to clear the road. The classic mid-career pattern. The work isn't more allies — it's converting one of them upward.
Your map has more weight on the resistance side than the support side. You can still win — but it's a different campaign than you're probably running.
Fewer than half of your high-influence stakeholders are engaged with AI. Whether your change is directly about AI or not, this misalignment is going to show up. Better to surface it now.
Three or more people on your map are stance-unknown on AI. That's the first thing to fix. Most senior decisions now turn on AI dynamics; flying blind on those is expensive.
Most of the people on your map have relatively low influence on this outcome. You're operating below where the decision actually gets made.
Most people on your map score low on support. Cold rooms can be warmed, but not by repetition. Usually they need a different argument, framing, or sponsor.

Your map has the structural ingredients for success: at least one engaged champion, supportive allies, balance tilted in your favour, and reasonable visibility on where people sit. The risk in a healthy map is complacency. Champions get distracted. Stances shift. Map again in three months.

You don't have anyone in the top-right quadrant — someone senior enough to clear obstacles and supportive enough to actually do it. People without a champion don't fail dramatically. They just run out of road. The work: identify one person who could be a champion. Usually they're already in your allies quadrant — supportive but not yet senior enough, or senior but not yet engaged. The question is what would move them up.

More weight sits on the resistance side than the support side. You'll need either a stronger champion to outflank the resistance, or a strategy that doesn't depend on the resisters' agreement. For each person in the resistance quadrant, ask: are they opposing the change, or the way it's being driven? Different answers, different moves.

You have allies — people who like you and want this to work — but no one with the seniority to make it move. The work isn't more allies. It's converting one ally into a champion (typically by giving them a role in the change), or finding a sponsor outside your current cluster.

Most of the people on your map have relatively low influence on this outcome. Either you've mapped the wrong people, or — more likely — you're operating below the level where decisions actually get made. The change you're trying to drive will need new entries on this map, ones you don't currently have access to. Who's the actual decision-maker? Who do they listen to?

Most people on your map score low on support. Either you've underestimated them — worth checking — or this is a genuinely cold environment for the change you're driving. Cold rooms can be warmed, but not by repetition. They usually need a different argument, a different framing, or a different sponsor.

You've marked three or more people as Unknown on AI stance. The AI dimension of most senior decisions is increasingly the dimension that determines whether the decision lands — and you don't know where your stakeholders sit. The work, before anything else: find out. Not by asking them what they think about AI generally, which gets you talking points. By asking what specific changes they've made in the last six months in how they or their teams work.

Fewer than half of your high-influence stakeholders are actively engaged with AI. The change you're driving may or may not depend on AI directly — but if it touches operating model, headcount, technology investment, or speed of execution, it depends on it indirectly. An influential stakeholder who hasn't engaged with AI will reliably misread what you're proposing as either too aggressive or too timid. They won't know which, and neither will you, until you've done the alignment work.

Your strongest champion has a sceptical stance on AI. This is the most specific risk in your map. They'll support you up to the point where the AI dimension of your initiative becomes visible — and at that point they may stop, or worse, push back without realising the change is what they originally supported. The work: have the AI-specific conversation with them before you need their support on the AI-specific decision. If they're going to push back, you want to know now.

Your map has the structural ingredients for success. The risk in a healthy map is complacency. Champions get distracted. Allies move on. Stances shift, particularly on AI. Map this again in three months — and once a quarter after that, on whatever change you're driving at the time. The discipline of mapping is most of the value.
Want your map as a PDF, plus the False Friends question and a 90-day worksheet?
Or book a 90-minute stakeholder-mapping session →

seoTitle: Support Matrix Audit — Map Your Real Support Network — fab.partners
seoDescription: A four-minute exercise that most leadership teams should have done six months ago. Map the support network around the change you're driving.
